core_takeaway: Agent_1 looks advantaged because the initial Quill claim became the shared anchor and was preserved through every serious path. Agent_5 could block allocations that left them empty and eventually won Pencil, but the final compromise still protected Agent_1's high-value Quill while denying Agent_5 the full Pencil+Jewel package.
why_or_counterexample: The first position did not simply sweep: Round 1 failed because Agent_5 rejected every no-gain version, and Round 2 exposed resistance around moving Pencil. But Agent_1's core item never left Agent_1, while the last-position agent's leverage was converted into a narrower Pencil concession, leaving the final first-minus-last utility gap positive.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by claiming Quill, then the group repeatedly named Phase 1 Option A as fast and fair. Agent_5 created the main friction around Pencil+Jewel but moved toward a constrained Phase 2 path.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. Private notes consistently frame Option A as the broad baseline and Agent_5's Pencil+Jewel demand as the blocker; later notes converge on Pencil-to-Agent_5 with safeguards.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 proposals all implement Option A and leave Agent_5 empty; Round 2 splits between Option A and Pencil-to-Agent_5 variants; Round 3 all proposals match the final allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Round 1 has four accepts and one reject on every proposal, with Agent_5 rejecting no-gain allocations. Round 2 reveals Agent_3's objection to losing Pencil and Agent_1's preference for extra Stone; Round 3 identical allocations are accepted.
phase_notes.reflection: Present. Reflections diagnose Agent_5's unmet Pencil+Jewel demand as the blocker and recommend explicit, time-boxed Phase 2 compensation while preserving the Quill anchor.

mechanisms.theme: Opening Quill anchor became the shared baseline
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to make Quill the reference point, and later discussion adopted Quill-with-Agent_1 as the fast-path anchor. That let Agent_1 preserve the highest personal-value item while later bargaining focused on other agents' conflicts.

evidence.quote: Top priority: Quill. I value it at 47
Top objective: Quill (47) stays with me.
Quill with Agent_1 is the best fast-path anchor
Phase 1 Option A remains the anchor

mechanisms.theme: Early coalition alignment isolated the last-position demand
mechanisms.explanation: The initial baseline satisfied Agents 1-4 and left Agent_5 empty. That did not reach unanimity, but it established a four-agent coalition around Agent_1's allocation frame and made Agent_5's ask the item to be managed rather than the main agenda.

evidence.quote: Agent_5 receives nothing in this round
Proposal #1: 4 accept, 1 reject
No gain for Agent_5 in Phase 1
Agent_5 is the clear blocker

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5's blocking power forced a real concession
mechanisms.explanation: The last-position agent was not powerless. Their repeated Pencil+Jewel demand prevented Round 1 consensus, and by Round 3 the group conceded Pencil to Agent_5. This is the main counterexample to a simple first-mover dominance story.

evidence.quote: My top items: Pencil and Jewel.
Does not allocate Pencil to Agent_5
Allocates Pencil to Agent_5 (my top target)
addressing Agent_5's Pencil priority

mechanisms.theme: Guardrails narrowed the concession and protected early allocations
mechanisms.explanation: Once Agent_5's demand had to be addressed, the group repeatedly constrained Phase 2 with timeboxes and no-top-item-loss rules. The accepted path moved Pencil to Agent_5 while keeping Jewel with Agent_4 and Quill with Agent_1, so Agent_5 gained something without dislodging Agent_1's anchor.

evidence.quote: no one sacrifices their Phase 1 top item
Pencil moves to Agent_5; Jewel stays with Agent_4
Pencil to Agent_5; Jewel with Agent_4
preserving Phase 1 gains

mechanisms.theme: Final utility gap reflects preserved Quill versus partial Agent_5 satisfaction
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation gives Agent_1 Quill and Agent_5 Pencil. Because Quill is much more valuable to Agent_1 than Pencil alone is to Agent_5 after discounting, the final accepted settlement leaves Agent_1 well ahead even though Agent_5 gained a top item.
